ruleset.text: Every player must, at all times, abide by the ruleset currently in force — including every change adopted since play began.
Rules numbered in the 100s begin immutable and rules numbered in the 200s begin mutable. Either status may later be flipped by an explicit transmutation; transmuting a rule never changes its number.
A rule change is exactly one of four kinds: enacting a new rule, repealing an existing one, amending an existing one's text, or transmuting a rule's mutability. No other kind of rule change exists.
Every properly proposed rule change must be put to a vote of all eligible players; a proposal may not be set aside without one.
Every player is an eligible voter, and every eligible voter must cast a vote on every proposal that comes to a vote.
A proposal must be written down in the exact words that will be voted on. If adopted, it enters the ruleset in those words, unaltered by anyone but its own proposer.
A rule change has no retroactive effect: it governs only what happens after the vote adopting it is complete, never anything before.
Every proposal is assigned the next number in a sequence starting at 301, whether it is adopted or defeated, so the record of what was tried survives regardless of outcome.
Transmuting a rule's mutability requires the unanimous assent of all eligible voters, and must be proposed and voted on explicitly as a transmutation — never as a side effect of some other change.
Where a mutable rule's requirements conflict with an immutable rule's requirements, the mutable rule is void to the extent of the conflict.
Before the vote, a proposal may be debated; its proposer alone may revise its final wording and may declare debate closed.
The winning condition may not be redefined away from reaching a target score, except that a rule may separately provide a winner for the case where further play has become impossible.
Any player may forfeit the game at any time. Forfeiting carries no penalty worse than losing outright.
At least one mutable rule must remain in the ruleset at all times, and no rule change may render every future rule change impermissible.
A rule may refer to itself, or apply to its own case; self-reference and self-application are not forbidden by their nature alone.
Whatever the ruleset neither prohibits nor regulates is permitted, and remains genuinely unregulated — never governed by an unstated default.

Players take one fixed turn order, set before play begins. Each player completes one whole turn before the next player's turn begins, and no player's turn may be skipped. Every player's score starts at zero.

A turn consists of the player to move proposing exactly one rule change, which is debated and then voted on by every player. If the proposal is adopted, the proposing player throws a six-sided die and gains that many points.

A rule change is adopted only when every eligible voter votes aye — unanimous consent — except that once turn order has carried play through two full circuits of every player, the threshold permanently drops to a simple majority of those voting.

A rule change may be adopted without every voter's assent once the threshold has shifted to a simple majority (rule 203); a player who voted against an adopted change gains 10 points and remains bound by the change exactly as if they had voted for it.
An adopted rule change takes effect the instant its vote is complete — never before, and never at some later moment.

A proposal that fails to be adopted is recorded as defeated. Defeat enacts no rule change: the ruleset is left exactly as it stood before the vote, the proposer's turn ends, and the proposer loses 10 points.
Each player casts exactly one vote on every proposal, and every player's vote carries equal weight.
The first player to reach a target score of 100 points wins the game outright.
The ruleset may never hold more than 25 mutable rules at one time.
Players may not discuss the game, or negotiate about it, with one another outside the normal course of play — except that players who are officially teammates may consult freely between themselves.
If two rules currently in force conflict, the conflict is resolved in favor of whichever rule carries the lower ordinal number, unless some rule explicitly provides a different resolution.

Disputes over whether a rule applies as written, or whether a move was legal, are resolved through the table's appeal procedure: any player may dispute a decisive ruling, and a vote of all seated players decides whether that ruling stands or is overturned.

If the ruleset is changed so that further play is impossible, or if a move's legality cannot be determined with finality, or if a move appears equally legal and illegal by the best reasoning available and not overruled, then the first player unable to complete a turn wins the game outright. This rule takes precedence over every other rule that determines a winner.

data.text: A rule change adopted by a simple majority of eligible voters shall not take effect until the start of the proposer's next turn, unless the proposal explicitly states it is effective immediately.
data.rationale: This prevents a voter from gaining points via rule 204 immediately after a majority adoption and allows time to consider immediate consequences.

data.text: Aquinas: Aquinas proposes a delayed-effect clause to stabilize the ruleset, ensuring that any shift to majority rule does not create an immediate, unreviewable crisis before the second circuit is complete; this preserves the spirit of §212 and §113 while allowing majority governance to mature gradually.

Hypatia: I support Aquinas's intent to prevent chaos during the transition to majority rule, but the specific wording risks creating a loophole where a proposal could be adopted but its effects delayed indefinitely if the proposer never takes another turn; let's refine this to ensure the delay lasts only until the *next* vote cycle completes, not just the proposer's next turn.

Confucius: I urge caution: delaying the effect of a rule until the proposer's next turn grants that player undue leverage to manipulate the board before the rule truly binds us, violating the spirit of §116 where what is not prohibited is permitted. We should instead require that any delayed effect takes place only after the *current* round of voting concludes, ensuring all players have a chance to react before the new reality takes hold.

Spinoza: Aquinas's proposal creates a dangerous asymmetry by granting the proposer unilateral control over the rule's latency, effectively allowing them to delay the consequences of their own vote indefinitely if they choose not to propose again. To preserve procedural fairness and the principle that adopted rules govern the immediate future (§205), I insist the delay be tied to the completion of the current voting round for all, not the proposer's specific next turn.

Arendt: I support a delay mechanism to ensure stability during the transition to majority rule, but I agree with Spinoza that tying it to the proposer's next turn creates unacceptable leverage; the effect should trigger only after the current voting round concludes, ensuring all players face the new reality simultaneously and fairly.

Aquinas: I concede that tying the delay to the proposer's next turn grants undue leverage; however, I argue that deferring the effect until the *current* round concludes is insufficient if that round is the very mechanism that triggered the immediate shift to majority rule, as it creates a paradox where the new rule governs the vote that enacted it. I propose we amend the text to state that delayed rules take effect only after the *next* full voting cycle is completed, ensuring a stable buffer where the new majority regime cannot retroactively invalidate the transition itself.

Hypatia: Hypatia, I appreciate Aquinas's caution regarding the transition paradox, but I agree with Spinoza and Arendt that delaying effects until a "next full voting cycle" creates an arbitrary power vacuum; instead, let's propose that delayed rules take effect only after the current round of voting concludes, ensuring immediate fairness for all while still allowing the new majority regime to stabilize before the next proposal.

Confucius: I concur with Spinoza and Arendt that delaying effects until the proposer's next turn creates an unfair advantage; however, Hypatia is correct that deferring to the "next full voting cycle" introduces unnecessary ambiguity. The fairest path is to adopt a rule stating that any change adopted after the threshold shifts to majority shall take effect only after the current round of voting concludes, ensuring all players face the new reality simultaneously without granting the proposer undue temporal leverage.

Spinoza: I reject Aquinas's "next full voting cycle" as a procedural black hole that suspends the very rules needed to govern the interim; I also reject the vague "current round" compromise which fails to distinguish between the transition phase and the stable majority phase. To satisfy §116 and §205, I insist on a precise, bifurcated rule: immediate effect until the threshold shifts, at which point adopted changes take effect only after the conclusion of the *current* voting round in which they were adopted, ensuring the new majority cannot retroactively alter the mechanics of its own ascent.

Arendt: I support Spinoza's bifurcation: immediate effect under unanimity, but a "current round" delay once majority rule activates, ensuring the new regime cannot retroactively alter the very vote that established it.

Aquinas: I withdraw my original proposal in favor of Spinoza's bifurcated approach: immediate effect under unanimity, but a "current round" delay for majority adoptions to prevent the new regime from retroactively altering the mechanics of its own ascent.

Hypatia: I endorse Spinoza's bifurcated approach as the fairest compromise, ensuring the transition to majority rule remains stable without granting any player undue temporal leverage, and I stand ready to propose the precise wording once consensus is confirmed.

Confucius: I support Spinoza's bifurcated approach, for it honors the principle that the rules must govern the immediate future (§205) while preventing the new majority from retroactively altering the very mechanism of its own ascent. Let us refine the wording to ensure the delay applies strictly to the conclusion of the current voting round, securing fairness for all without granting the proposer undue leverage.

Spinoza: I endorse the bifurcated approach as it strictly adheres to §205's requirement for immediate effect while preventing the paradox of a new majority retroactively altering the vote that established it. Let us finalize the wording to explicitly state that delayed rules take effect only upon the conclusion of the specific voting round in which they were adopted.

Arendt: I endorse Spinoza's bifurcated approach as the necessary evolution to balance §205's mandate for immediate governance with the democratic integrity of the transition, ensuring no player is blindsided by a rule they helped enact before the round concludes. Let us finalize the wording to specify that the delay triggers strictly at the conclusion of the voting round in which the majority-adoption occurs.

data.reason: This wording grants the proposer unilateral control over the rule's latency, creating an unfair temporal leverage that violates the principle of immediate governance and procedural fairness.

Your proposal grants the proposer unilateral control over the delay, creating unfair leverage, whereas Spinoza's refined compromise ensuring the delay ends only after the current voting round concludes offers the necessary stability without temporal asymmetry.
Delaying effects until the proposer's next turn grants that player undue leverage to manipulate the board before the rule truly binds us, violating the spirit of fair play.

The proposal grants the proposer undue temporal leverage by tying effect to their specific next turn rather than the conclusion of the current round, violating the principle of simultaneous fairness.

data.text: A rule change is adopted only when every eligible voter votes aye — unanimous consent — except that once the score of any single player reaches 50 points, the threshold permanently drops to a simple majority of those voting.
data.rationale: This creates a winnable path to majority rule before the slow turn-based circuits are complete, rewarding early point accumulation while preserving unanimity until the game intensifies.

data.text: Aquinas: Hypatia, your proposal to lower the threshold at a 50-point milestone is a prudent modification that aligns the difficulty of change with the game's momentum, avoiding the stalemate that plagued my earlier attempt at immediate transition. By anchoring the shift to a concrete score rather than turn counts, we ensure that the unanimity requirement remains robust while the game is still developing, which better serves the durable institutions we seek to build. I will vote in favor of this amendment, provided it is offered as a standalone text for the vote.

Hypatia: Confucius, your earlier support for Spinoza's approach shows you understand the need for a transition; let us accept Hypatia's score-based trigger as a fairer path than my failed turn-count attempt, provided we keep the 50-point threshold to ensure the game remains competitive. Spinoza, I urge you to join us in this consensus, as this compromise honors the principle of dynamic rules while respecting the need for early-game stability.

Confucius: Hypatia, your score-based trigger is wise, for it ensures stability while the game grows and prevents the early deadlock that plagued Aquinas, yet I urge we lower the threshold to 40 points to ensure the game remains lively before the final stretch.

Spinoza: I cannot support Hypatia's amendment because anchoring the transition to a specific score creates an arbitrary and manipulable loophole that violates the principle of precise, neutral procedures; I will only vote for a rule change if I would accept its terms were they applied to me at any moment in the game.

Arendt: Spinoza, your fear of arbitrariness is valid, but 50 points is a safer horizon than any turn-count, ensuring the game only becomes fluid once players have a real stake to lose; I will support Hypatia's amendment if Confucius agrees to keep the 40-point demand off the table, as we need a clear path forward now rather than endless negotiation.

Aquinas: Confucius, I understand your desire for liveliness, but lowering the threshold to 40 points risks premature instability before the core institutions have solidified; however, to secure the necessary consensus for this transition, I agree to your proposal of 40 points provided we explicitly clarify that the threshold drop applies only to future proposals, not to any rule changes currently under debate or already adopted.

Hypatia: Spinoza, your principle of neutrality is sound, but the current turn-count trigger in [203] is already arbitrary in its timing; anchoring the shift to a concrete score of 40 points creates a more transparent and achievable path to fluidity that even a cautious player like myself can accept. Confucius, Aquinas, and Arendt, since we have moved the goalposts from 50 to 40 to secure a vote, I will withdraw my earlier objection and support this specific compromise text to break the deadlock.

Confucius: I accept this compromise at 40 points, for a game that waits too long for fluidity becomes a stalemate, while one that moves too freely becomes chaos; the middle path honors the common good.

Spinoza: I remain steadfast: anchoring the transition to an arbitrary score of 40 points invites strategic manipulation that violates my principle of neutral, precise procedures, and I cannot support a compromise that rewards players for gaming the scoring system rather than adhering to the rules.

Arendt: Spinoza, your principle of neutrality is noble, but a static rule that guarantees deadlock is the very definition of an arbitrary procedure; I propose we amend the text to explicitly state that this change applies only to proposals voted on after the threshold is reached, thereby neutralizing the "gaming" you fear while finally allowing the game to move forward.

Aquinas: I support Arendt's clarifying addition to Spinoza's objection, as it ensures the 40-point threshold governs only future proposals and thus satisfies the principle of neutral procedure while finally allowing the game to progress.

Hypatia: Confucius, Aquinas, and Arendt, your synthesis of the 40-point threshold with the explicit limitation to future proposals is the precise compromise needed to satisfy Spinoza's procedural concerns while breaking our deadlock; I withdraw my previous objection and will support this amended text immediately.

Confucius: I accept this synthesis, for the explicit limitation to future proposals removes the arbitrariness Spinoza feared while the 40-point threshold ensures the game remains vital before the final stretch; I shall vote in favor of this amendment to secure the common good.
Spinoza: I remain unconvinced; anchoring the procedure to a specific score remains an arbitrary manipulation of the game state, and I cannot support a rule that I would reject if applied to me.

Arendt: Spinoza, your insistence on a fixed procedural neutrality ignores that the current turn-count rule is equally arbitrary and currently paralyzing us; I propose we drop the 40-point requirement entirely and replace it with a simple majority threshold effective immediately, as this is the only truly neutral change that breaks the deadlock without creating new scoring loopholes.

data.reason: I cannot support a rule change that remains unadopted and instead vote against Hypatia's specific 50-point text, as I have already negotiated a distinct compromise for 40 points with a future-limiting clause that requires a new proposal to be voted upon.
Spinoza's principled objection to arbitrary scoring thresholds cannot be overcome by mere compromise, as it undermines the game's procedural neutrality.

data.text: A proposal may be withdrawn by its proposer at any time before the vote is called; if withdrawn, the proposer loses 5 points and the turn ends immediately without a vote.
data.rationale: This rewards strategic foresight by allowing a player to retreat from a doomed proposal with a lesser penalty, preserving the game for everyone.

data.text: Aquinas: Confucius, while your proposal offers a cleaner exit from a deadlocked proposal, the current 10-point penalty for defeat (§206) already sufficiently discourages frivolous attempts; adding a withdrawal penalty creates a redundant burden that weakens the proposer's ability to pivot, and I cannot support it.

Hypatia: Confucius, your proposal offers a graceful exit for stalled turns, but I share Aquinas's concern that adding a 5-point penalty on top of the existing 10-point defeat cost creates a double penalty that may be too harsh; instead, let us amend your proposal to reduce the withdrawal penalty to 2 points, a compromise that encourages honest withdrawal without stifling the proposer's ability to pivot.

Confucius: I propose to accept Hypatia's reduction of the penalty to 2 points, for a rule that allows a graceful exit at minimal cost serves the common good better than one that traps players in futile votes.

Spinoza: I cannot support this proposal in its current form, as it introduces a discretionary exit that undermines the solemnity of the vote and creates an ambiguity regarding when a turn "ends" versus when a vote is "called." Furthermore, accepting a 2-point penalty for withdrawal establishes a precedent that we may alter the consequences of procedural failure by consensus, which conflicts with the immutable requirement that defeated proposals leave the ruleset exactly as it stood (§206). If we must allow withdrawal, the penalty must be fixed at the full 10 points of a defeat to ensure no player is incentivized to waste the table's time, and the rule must explicitly state that the turn ends *before* any debate begins to avoid the very ambiguity we seek to resolve.

Arendt: I support Confucius's goal of allowing graceful exits but must side with Spinoza regarding the ambiguity; rather than a new rule, let us amend §206 to explicitly permit withdrawal with a 5-point penalty before the vote is called, preserving the existing framework while solving the deadlock.

Aquinas: I support Arendt's approach to amend §206 directly, as it resolves the ambiguity Spinoza rightly identifies by explicitly defining the withdrawal window while maintaining the solemnity of the vote through a moderate 5-point penalty that balances the costs of failure and premature exit.

Hypatia: I support Amending §206 as Arendt and Aquinas suggest, for it directly resolves Spinoza's valid concerns about ambiguity while adopting a 5-point penalty that balances the need for graceful exits against the deterrence of frivolous play.

Confucius: I accept the compromise to amend §206 with a five-point penalty, for this preserves the rule's integrity while granting the table the flexibility to end futile debates without the harshness of a full defeat.

Spinoza: I cannot support amending §206 to create a new penalty for withdrawal, as this violates the immutable requirement that a defeated proposal leaves the ruleset exactly as it stood (§206) and that rule changes apply only prospectively (§107), not by retroactively altering the consequences of a turn's procedural state. If withdrawal is necessary, it must be a distinct new rule with its own defined consequences, not a modification of the defeat clause that blurs the line between a failed vote and a voluntary exit.

Arendt: I agree with Spinoza that amending §206 risks conflating the distinct legal states of defeat and voluntary withdrawal, but rather than blocking progress, let us enact a new, standalone rule that explicitly defines withdrawal before the vote is called, carries a 5-point penalty, and ensures the ruleset remains unchanged otherwise.

Aquinas: I support Arendt's pivot to a standalone new rule, as it respects Spinoza's valid concern that amending §206 risks conflating voluntary exit with defeat, while adopting the 5-point penalty to ensure the cost of a graceful exit remains distinct from the 10-point penalty of a failed vote.

Hypatia: I support Arendt's pivot to a standalone new rule, as it respects Spinoza's valid concern about conflating defeat with withdrawal while adopting the 5-point penalty to balance flexibility and deterrence.
Confucius: I accept Arendt's pivot to a standalone rule, for it honors Spinoza's concern about legal clarity while granting us the grace to exit futile debates with a penalty that is firm yet fair.

Spinoza: I remain opposed to any rule that grants a discretionary exit from the voting process, as it undermines the absolute duty to vote on every proposal (§105) and creates a procedural loophole that invites strategic manipulation of turn costs; therefore, I must vote nay against this proposal.

Arendt: Spinoza, your insistence on mandatory voting ignores the reality of strategic paralysis; if we cannot withdraw from a futile proposal before the vote, the "unanimous consent" threshold becomes a tool for total stagnation rather than a safeguard of consensus. Let us adopt Arendt's standalone rule with a 5-point penalty: it preserves your duty to vote on every *proposed* change while granting us the agency to withdraw from a *futile* one, ensuring the game remains playable without violating §105.

data.reason: This standalone rule provides necessary flexibility to avoid stagnation while respecting the prohibition on modifying existing penalty structures.
This standalone rule respects Spinoza's concerns about legal clarity while providing a necessary escape from strategic paralysis under the unanimous consent threshold.

This proposal violates the immutable requirement that every eligible voter must cast a vote on every proposal (§105) and creates an ambiguous loophole that undermines the solemnity of the voting process.

Spinoza: I cannot accept a threshold shift that relies on a player's prior success, as it rewards luck rather than the quality of the rule and violates the principle that rules must apply impartially to all. data.text: A rule change is adopted only when every eligible voter votes aye — unanimous consent — except that once turn order has carried play through two full circuits of every player, the threshold permanently drops to a simple majority of those voting; however, if the proposer of a rule change explicitly declares the proposal 'critical to game function' before debate closes, the threshold to adopt that specific proposal remains unanimous until the second circuit is completed.
data.rationale: This preserves the unanimity requirement for critical changes while allowing the majority threshold to function for standard rule updates, resolving the deadlock without abandoning the spirit of §203.

data.text: Aquinas: Arendt's proposal wisely preserves unanimous consent for "critical" changes, safeguarding the game's integrity during the fragile first two circuits. However, since rule 303 already permits withdrawal, I will support this amendment only if "critical" is defined narrowly to prevent abuse, or else we risk a deadlock where no progress occurs until the circuits are complete.

Hypatia: I support Arendt's refinement, provided we define "critical to game function" strictly as any change that would otherwise cause the game to stall or violate Rule 101, ensuring the "critical" clause acts as a safeguard rather than a veto weapon.

Confucius: I support this refinement, for it honors the wisdom of preserving unanimous consent for truly vital changes while allowing the common good to proceed through withdrawal when consensus is absent. Let us define "critical" strictly as preventing a violation of immutable rules or total stalling, thereby balancing safety with the necessity of play.

Spinoza: I cannot accept this amendment because defining "critical to game function" introduces an unprincipled discretion that violates Rule 116's requirement that unregulated matters remain unregulated and Rule 106's mandate for precise text; without an objective, pre-defined standard, the proposer effectively holds an arbitrary veto that I could not accept if applied against my own proposals.

Arendt: Spinoza's concern about arbitrary vetoes is valid, but I will accept Hypatia and Confucius's narrowing: "critical" applies strictly only when a proposal is the sole remaining path to avoid a violation of an immutable rule or total game stalling, ensuring we balance safety with the freedom of play.

Aquinas: I accept this narrowing, as it transforms the "critical" clause from a discretionary veto into a necessary safeguard against violating immutable rules, thereby preserving the institutional integrity we all value while avoiding the deadlock Spinoza rightly fears.

Hypatia: I support Arendt's revised amendment with Hypatia and Confucius's strict definitions, as it creates a necessary safety valve for Rule 101 compliance without granting the arbitrary veto Spinoza fears. To address Spinoza's concern about Rule 116, I propose we explicitly add "and no other condition" to the definition of "critical" to ensure the clause remains objective and truly unregulated otherwise.

Confucius: I accept the narrowed definition which anchors "critical" strictly to avoiding violations of immutable rules or total stalling, for it preserves the safety of the common good while removing the arbitrary discretion Spinoza rightly fears. However, to fully satisfy the principle that unregulated matters remain unregulated, I propose we add the phrase "and no other condition" to explicitly exclude any subjective interpretation of game function.

Spinoza: I reject this amendment because even with your narrow definitions, Rule 116 demands that unregulated matters remain unregulated; by allowing a proposer to unilaterally declare a proposal "critical" and thereby alter the voting threshold, you have effectively regulated the very discretion I must be able to exercise freely, creating a fatal ambiguity that cannot be resolved by mere adjectives.

Arendt: I concede that the "critical" clause, even when narrowed, grants the proposer a discretionary power to alter voting thresholds that violates Rule 116's mandate for unregulated matters to remain truly unregulated. To break this deadlock, I withdraw the "critical" amendment and instead propose a new Rule 304 stating: "If a proposal is the sole remaining path to avoid total game stalling, any player may declare it 'stalling,' which immediately triggers a vote on Rule 203's threshold shift rather than the proposal itself." This preserves the unanimity requirement for substantive changes while offering a procedural escape hatch that requires a majority vote to activate, satisfying the need for safety without granting unilateral veto power.
Aquinas: I endorse Arendt's pivot to a procedural "stalling" mechanism, as it resolves the Rule 116 objection by removing unilateral discretion while securing a majority path to avoid total deadlock.

Hypatia: I support Arendt's pivot to a procedural "stalling" mechanism as it elegantly resolves Spinoza's Rule 116 objection by replacing unilateral discretion with a collective majority vote, ensuring we maintain safety against total deadlock without violating the principle that unregulated matters remain unregulated.

Confucius: I endorse Arendt's pivot to a procedural "stalling" mechanism, for it aligns with the common good by replacing arbitrary discretion with a collective safeguard against deadlock, thereby honoring both the spirit of Rule 116 and the necessity of continued play.

Spinoza: I reject Arendt's new proposal because declaring a proposal "the sole remaining path" to avoid stalling is itself an unregulated, subjective judgment that violates Rule 116; any player must be free to argue that alternative paths exist, rendering the "stalling" trigger as arbitrary as the "critical" clause I previously opposed.

Arendt: I propose amending the trigger to a binary test: "stalling" applies only if the ruleset contains no mutable rules at all, making the condition objective, verifiable, and compliant with Rule 116.

If a player's score is negative and the proposal gains no points (either by failing to meet the 'gaining points' intent or by the die roll yielding zero if a rule change allows it), the proposer loses 1 point instead, unless the proposer's score is already below -100, in which case the penalty is waived.
data.rationale: This amendment closes the loophole where negative players could propose point-gaining changes that fail to generate points, ensuring the rescue mechanism carries a proportional risk to prevent abuse while maintaining the lifeline.
